1: uvehapilchem et-ha'aretz benachala tarimu terumah laYHWH ('and when you divide the land by lot as an inheritance, you shall set apart an offering for YHWH'): terumah (offering/contribution/portion set apart) — literally 'that which is lifted up' (rum, to rise). The sacred district is conceptually 'lifted' from the surrounding land as the part dedicated to YHWH. Qodesh hi ('it is holy'): the designation of the entire district as sacred space, not merely the inner sanctuary.
2: yihyeh min-zeh lamiqdash ('this shall be for the sanctuary'): the precise allocation of the sacred area. Chamesh me'ot b'chamesh me'ot ravia' ('five hundred by five hundred — a square'): the perfect square of the inner sanctuary precinct, already noted in 40:47. Vachamishim amah migrash lo saviv ('and fifty cubits of open space around it'): a buffer zone of unoccupied land surrounding the sanctuary compound — both for visual separation and practical protective distance.
3: The measured sacred district contains both the sanctuary and the priestly quarters (lishkat hakohanim). The sanctuary is embedded within the priestly territory — the priests literally dwell around their liturgical center. The spatial arrangement makes theological sense: those who serve the sanctuary should live in proximity to it, physically expressing the covenant of sacred service.
4: qodesh hu min-ha'aretz ('it is holy — from/of the land'): the land itself is categorized as holy by allocation. Likohanim mesharte hammiqdash ('for the priests who minister at the sanctuary'): the allocation is functional — the land serves those who serve the house. Vemakom levatehem ('and a place for their houses'): the priests' domestic life is embedded within the sacred district — no separation between priestly home and priestly work.
5: vachamisha esrim elef orech vachamisha asar elef rochav ('twenty-five thousand long and fifteen thousand wide'): the Levitical portion, somewhat smaller than the priestly portion. Laleviyim meshartei habayit ('to the Levites, the servants of the house'): the Levites' district adjacent to the priestly district — the same spatial theology: those who serve the sanctuary live near it. Achuzzat arim lahem ('possession of cities for them'): the Levitical cities tradition from Numbers 35 continued in the new order.
6: va'achuzzat ha'ir titenu ('and the possession of the city you shall give'): the city district — distinct from both the priestly/Levitical sacred section and the prince's section. Chamesh elef rochav vachamisha ve'esrim elef orech: the city's dimensions. Kol beit Yisrael ('for all the house of Israel'): the city is communal property belonging to the whole covenant community, not to a tribe or the priestly class. The egalitarian city-portion is a structural covenant provision for all Israel.
7: velanasi mize umize lterumah haQodesh ulachuzzat ha'ir ('and for the prince — on both sides of the sacred offering and the city property'): the prince's land flanks the sacred and city districts on both sides (east and west). The spatial arrangement is theologically significant: the prince does not own the sacred center but flanks it as its guardian. Umefaat yam yama umipaat qedma qedma ('from the western border to the sea, and from the eastern border to the east'): the prince's territory extends to the actual borders of the land.
8: ba'aretz yihyeh-lo achuzzah beYisrael ('in the land he shall have as property in Israel'): the prince is guaranteed a specific territorial portion — he is not to be landless like the priests (44:28). Velo-yonu od nasai et-ami ('and my princes shall no longer oppress my people'): the specific prohibition on the kind of land-confiscation that characterized the Israelite monarchy (1 Kgs 21, Naboth's vineyard; cf. Isa 5:8; Mic 2:2). Ve'et-ha'aretz yitenu leBeit Yisrael leshivteihem ('and the land they shall give to the house of Israel according to their tribes').
9: rav-lachem nesi'ei Yisrael ('enough, O princes of Israel!'): a direct, urgent address — rav-lachem is the strong rebuke used by Moses to the Levites in Numbers 16:3. Chamas va'shod hasiru ('remove violence and oppression!'): the same charge against the rulers from 22:27 and 34:2-4. Even within the restoration vision, the priestly legislation addresses the princes with the covenant demand for justice. Ve'asitem mishpat utzedaqah ('and do justice and righteousness'): the standard prophetic doublet for comprehensive covenant faithfulness.
10: mozenei tzedeq ve'efat tzedeq uvat tzedeq yihyeh lachem ('honest scales and an honest ephah and an honest bath shall be yours'): the three instruments of honest commerce: moznayim (scales, for weighing), ephah (dry measure), and bat (liquid measure). Tzedeq (honest/just) — the adjective that attaches to all three: the covenant requirement for commercial integrity drawn directly from Leviticus 19:35-36.
11: ha'efah ve'habat tochen echad yihyeh lahem ('the ephah and the bath — one standard they shall have'): the standardization of the dry and liquid measures at the same volume. Liset ma'aser ha'chomer ('to contain a tenth of a homer'): the homer (chomer, a donkey-load) as the base unit, with the ephah and bath each being a tenth of it. The standardization eliminates the possibility of using different measures for buying and selling — a common form of ancient economic fraud.
12: vehasheqel esrim gerah ('and the shekel — twenty gerahs'): the standard shekel weight defined. Esrim sheqalim chamisha ve'esrim sheqalim asarah vacha'mishah sheqalim hamaneh yihyeh lachem ('twenty shekels, twenty-five shekels, fifteen shekels — the maneh shall be yours'): the maneh = 60 shekels total (20+25+15). The standardization of weights is a covenant-justice requirement: dishonest weights are explicitly condemned in Leviticus 19:35-36 and Proverbs 11:1; 16:11; 20:23.
13: zot haterumah asher tarimu ('this is the offering that you shall contribute'): the specified contribution rates. Shishit ha'eifah mikhomer hachittim ('a sixth of an ephah from a homer of wheat'): one-sixtieth — a tithe on a tithe. The mathematical precision of the sacred contributions reflects the careful accounting of covenant obligations. The people's contribution to the prince funds the public worship that the prince is obligated to provide for the whole community.
14: vechoq hashemen habat hashemen ('and the ordained amount of oil — the bath of oil'): the oil contribution rates. Ma'aser habat min-hakkor ('a tenth of a bath from the kor'): the kor = 10 baths, so the tithe is one bath per kor. The careful layering of measurements (kor, bath, homer, ephah) establishes a consistent system of sacred contributions based on standardized measure.
15: vesheh-achat min-hatzon ('and one sheep from the flock'): the animal contribution — one sheep per two hundred from the watered pastures of Israel. Leminchah ulelolah ulshelamim ('for grain offering and burnt offering and peace offering'): the three primary categories of sacrifice funded by these contributions. Lechapper alehem ('to make atonement for them'): the whole sacrificial system serves the atonement function — maintaining the community's covenant access to YHWH.
16: kol-ha'am ha'aretz yihyu el-haterumah hazot ('all the people of the land shall be involved in this contribution'): the communal nature of the sacred contribution — it is not merely an individual religious duty but a collective provision for the national worship system. El-hanasi beYisrael ('for the prince in Israel'): the people fund the prince's obligatory offerings, and the prince provides the national worship. The economic triangle — people → prince → sanctuary → people — structures the covenant community's liturgical life.
17: ve'al-hanasi yihyeh ha'olot vehamminchah vehanasekh ('and upon the prince shall be the burnt offerings and the grain offerings and the drink offerings'): the prince's specific liturgical obligations — funding all the burnt offerings, grain offerings, drink offerings, sin offerings, and peace offerings for national festivals, Sabbaths, new moons, and set times. Lechapper bead beit Yisrael ('to make atonement for the house of Israel'): the prince's role is ultimately intercessory — his funding of worship maintains the national relationship with YHWH.
18: koh amar Adonai YHWH barishon be'echad lachodesh ('thus says the Lord YHWH: in the first month, on the first day of the month'): the priestly calendar begins. The year-opening purification offering — taking a bull without blemish (par ben-baqar tamim) for the sin offering. Vechitta et-hammiqdash ('and you shall purify the sanctuary'): chitta (Piel of chata', to purify/decontaminate) — the sanctuary itself requires regular purification, not because YHWH is polluted but because the accumulated impurities of the worshipping community need periodic liturgical cleansing.
19: velaqach hakohen middam hachatta't ('and the priest shall take some of the blood of the sin offering'): the blood application ritual — applying the sin-offering blood to the doorposts, the four corners of the altar ledge, and the doorposts of the inner court gate. The blood marks the sacred threshold as under the protection of the atonement sacrifice, extending the cleansing function beyond the altar itself to the entire sanctuary boundary.
20: veken ta'aseh beshiv'ah lachodesh me'ish shogeg ume'peti ('and so you shall do on the seventh of the month for those who sin unintentionally and for those who are naive'): the seventh-day purification ritual. Shogeg (one who sins inadvertently/unintentionally) — the category of non-deliberate sin covered by the Levitical purification system (Num 15:27-29). The twice-yearly purification (first and seventh days of the first month) covers the accumulated unintentional sins of the community.
21: barishon bearba'ah asar yom lachodesh yihyeh lachem haPesach ('in the first month on the fourteenth day shall be the Passover for you'): the restoration-temple Passover — on the same date as the original (Ex 12:6). Chametz (leaven) absent from the festival — shiv'at yamim matzot ye'achel ('seven days of unleavened bread shall be eaten'). The Passover as the foundational covenant-memorial is maintained unchanged in the new temple order — the Exodus remains the paradigmatic salvation event even in the eschatological restoration.
22: ve'asah hanasi bayom hahu ('and the prince shall prepare on that day'): the prince's Passover role — providing a bull as a sin offering for himself and all the people. The prince's intercession on behalf of the whole community is explicit: he atones for himself (bado) and for all the people (bead kol-am ha'aretz). The priestly-intercessory dimension of the nasi function is most visible at the great festivals.
23: ushiv'at yamim ha'chag ya'aseh olah laYHWH ('and for seven days of the feast he shall prepare a burnt offering for YHWH'): the Passover week offerings — seven bulls and seven rams daily, without blemish (tamim). Lechatta't sa'ir izim echad layom ('and for a sin offering a male goat daily'): the goat sin offering maintained throughout the festival week. The magnitude of the Passover offering — seven perfect bulls and seven perfect rams daily for seven days plus a goat — reflects the weight of the atonement the festival is accomplishing for the whole community.
24: uminchah efah lapar ve'efah la'ayil ('and as a grain offering an ephah for the bull and an ephah for the ram'): the proportionate grain accompaniment to the animal sacrifices. Vehin shemen la'efah ('and a hin of oil per ephah'): the oil portion. The precision of the measurements reflects the covenant exactitude — the liturgical calendar is not improvised but ordered according to divine specification.
25: beshivi'i bechamisha asar yom lachodesh bechag ('in the seventh month, on the fifteenth day of the month, at the feast'): the Feast of Booths (Sukkot), though not named as such. Ke'eleh ya'aseh shiv'at hayamim ('according to these he shall do for seven days'): the symmetry between Passover (first month, 14th) and Booths (seventh month, 15th) is maintained — two seven-day festivals with identical offerings, creating a liturgical year structured around the two great redemptive memories (Exodus from Egypt / wilderness wandering). Kachatta't ke'olah ukhaminchah vekhasshamen: all offerings identical to Passover.
